next_steps: Submit the whole-object Digitisation Services request with all binding and leaf surfaces specified.
Identify the poem at ff. 35r-46v separately and request color from the original where feasible.
Obtain written permission terms before placing supplied images in Git or print-on-demand outputs.
openQuestions.question: Will the British Library supply or publish a complete color facsimile of Harley MS 2274, and what redistribution terms would attach?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian Imaging Services whether a complete existing surrogate or new whole-object order is feasible.
Request all composite and binding surfaces and map the poem at ff. 192v-205 separately.
Compare Baker's five additional stanzas against the complete images.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete color facsimile of Bodleian MS Bodley 423 be obtained when the public object exposes only five non-overlapping leaves?

next_steps: Ask Corpus archives whether World Microfilms, Bodleian, or the College can supply an existing complete MS 261 surrogate and quote new whole-object imaging if not.
Ask TCD to confirm whether Literature reel 7 covers all of MS 160 and quote a complete color object if it does not.
Map the work only after folio labels and image inventories are verified.
openQuestions.question: Do complete existing digital or microfilm surrogates of Corpus Christi College Oxford MS 261 and Trinity College Dublin MS 160 exist, and what exactly do they cover?

next_steps: Compare every italic span against a color facsimile of Harley ff. 35r-46v.
Check whether Garrett documented his conventions elsewhere or in surviving papers.
Do not infer original letterforms from CME HI markup alone.
openQuestions.question: What manuscript forms do Garrett's 72 printed italic spans represent: expanded abbreviations, supplied letters, special signs, or more than one editorial function?

next_steps: Inspect all four witnesses and Baker's articles.
Submit a DIMEV correction with witness evidence if appropriate.
openQuestions.question: Is DIMEV 1.0's 'My sinful language to abate' a transcription/data error for 'My sinful living to abate'?

next_steps: Obtain complete surrogates and collate all four witnesses.
Review Baker 1969 and 1983 in full and current scholarship before proposing a stemma.
openQuestions.question: How are the 76-, 72-, 52-, and three-stanza copies textually related, and which readings reflect authorial development, scribal adaptation, or loss?

next_steps: Keep all source caches Git-ignored and publish links/locators only unless a component-specific license supports redistribution.
Treat Bodleian CC BY-NC images as noncommercial and verify each exact object; obtain separate written permissions from BL, Corpus, and TCD for supplied images.
Do not transfer METS XML's license to its PDF or commentary.
openQuestions.question: Which manuscript-image and modern-edition files may be redistributed in the public Git repository or commercial/noncommercial print-on-demand outputs?

reviewNotes: The cataloged medieval work is the anonymous Middle English poem. Garrett's modern-English preface is encoded editorial paratext, not part of the poem.
Garrett's foliation 35a-46b corresponds to the current BL locator ff. 35r-46v.
Garrett's ababababbcbC makes the refrain line typographically distinct as capital C; DIMEV's ababababbcbc expresses the rhyme sounds and is not contradictory.

Bodleian's five-image object is complete only as a capture of that provider-published selection. Its images are fols. 228r, 355r, 401v, 402v, and 409v, with zero overlap with the poem at ff. 192v-205.

Garrett dated the Harley hand and language to the very end of the fifteenth or beginning of the sixteenth century; the current BL parent record dates the manuscript to 1450-1499. Both are retained as attributed claims.
DIMEV 1.0's incipit says 'My sinful language to abate,' while Garrett, CME00093, and the BL title use living/lyuyng; the discrepancy remains open.

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00093 XML is a repository copy of the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item derived from Robert Max Garrett's complete article 'De Arte Lacrimandi,' Anglia 32 (1909), pp. 269-294. U-M retained Garrett's substantive four-paragraph preface, his 72-stanza Middle English text, manuscript-folio milestones, and apparatus. Garrett directly transcribed only British Library Harley MS 2274, historical ff. 35a-46b / current ff. 35r-46v. DIMEV 3777 adds three collateral witnesses—Bodleian MS Bodley 423, Corpus Christi College Oxford MS 261, and Trinity College Dublin MS 160—and later Baker scholarship; none is made a retroactive source of Garrett's text. Complete scans of the Anglia volume, DIMEV release, Abbott's Trinity catalog, and Saupe's supporting METS edition are cached. Digital Bodleian's complete published selection for Bodley 423 is also cached, but it contains only five images, none overlapping the poem. No publicly obtainable whole-manuscript facsimile was verified for any of the four witnesses, so whole-object request routes are recorded separately from work locators.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Fifteenth century; copied in Bodleian MS Bodley 423 by approximately 1430-1440
catalogSummary.region: England; precise place of composition and dialect region unresolved
catalogSummary.title: De Arte Lacrimandi

catalogingSubject.label: De Arte Lacrimandi
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English Marian poem conventionally called De Arte Lacrimandi, not Robert Max Garrett's four-paragraph English preface, the complete 1909 Anglia article, the U-M digital encoding, or any one physical manuscript. CME00093 retains both Garrett's editorial preface and his 72-stanza text, so those layers are modeled separately.

contentParts.label: Garrett's editorial preface
notes: This part is represented to disclose the CME item boundary; it is not part of the medieval work cataloged as De Arte Lacrimandi.
contentParts.summary: Four modern-English prose paragraphs on Harley MS 2274, metre and rhyme, linguistic features, and literary construction; printed on pp. 269-270 and outside the medieval poem's intellectual boundary.

contentParts.label: De Arte Lacrimandi
contentParts.summary: Garrett's complete 72-stanza text of the anonymous Middle English poem, with manuscript-folio milestones and notes on Harley readings and corrections.

assertion.notes: The normalized range is a cautious inference from the earliest currently identified copy, not an independently dated act of composition.

dateStatements.display: Fifteenth century; necessarily no later than the approximately 1430-1440 Bodley 423 copy

assertion.notes: This dates the manuscript part containing the poem, not the poem's composition. The consulted Digital Bodleian selection describes the composite codex only broadly as mid-fifteenth century and does not image the poem.

dateStatements.display: Approximately 1430-1440 in Bodleian Library MS Bodley 423, part B

assertion.notes: Garrett is the earliest complete publication identified in the consulted edition and DIMEV bibliography; a comprehensive pre-1909 publication search was not performed.

dateStatements.display: 1909 in Robert Max Garrett's Anglia edition

extent.description: At the encoded-item boundary, 76 modeled items comprise four editorial-preface paragraphs and 72 medieval verse stanzas; the cataloged medieval work itself is the 72 LG stanzas and 864 L elements, twelve lines per stanza. CME00093 also has 74 HEAD elements across both layers, 26 PB elements covering Garrett's article pp. 269-294, 23 manuscript-folio milestones, 36 NOTE elements, 72 italic HI spans, and 12 SUP elements. These are encoding and edition metrics, not evidence that every witness has the same stanza count or typographic conventions.

formStatements.description: Seventy-two twelve-line stanzas, each ending in a variable form of the refrain 'Who can not wepe come lerne att me.'

assertion.notes: The penitential speaker kneels before an image of pity, is ravished out of the body, and hears the Virgin's first-person narrative. The visionary frame occupies the opening stanzas rather than the entire poem.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The brief Latin title and Garrett's modern English editorial preface do not make the medieval poem itself multilingual.

Garrett's printed rhyme scheme ababababbcbC uses capital C to mark the refrain line; DIMEV normalizes the sound pattern as ababababbcbc. These statements are compatible and do not imply a substantive disagreement.
Garrett's historical recto/verso notation 35a-46b is cross-walked to the current BL form ff. 35r-46v; no constant offset is involved.

The 1909 edition's estimate of very late fifteenth- or early sixteenth-century handwriting and language is retained in lineage as Garrett's historical judgment, while the current BL whole-manuscript record dates Harley MS 2274 to the second half of the fifteenth century.

next_steps: Review current linguistic scholarship across all four witnesses.
Collate the complete Bodley, Harley, Corpus, and Trinity texts before narrowing the work-level date or region.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's composition date, place, and dialect be established without transferring a surviving copy's production facts to the abstract work?

next_steps: Check the Harley, Bodley, Corpus, and Trinity readings directly.
Report a correction to DIMEV if all witnesses and historical editions support 'living.'
openQuestions.question: Is DIMEV 1.0's second incipit line 'My sinful language to abate' a data-entry error for Garrett and Harley's 'My sinful living to abate'?

next_steps: Compare refrain wording, visionary frame, and manuscript contexts without treating a shared refrain as proof of common authorship.
openQuestions.question: What textual or devotional relationship, if any, links De Arte Lacrimandi to the shorter same-refrain Marian lyric edited by Saupe?

assertion.notes: The Middle English work survives in English collections and Bodley 423 part B is assigned to England. This broad cultural origin does not establish a precise place of composition or transfer any one scribe's dialect to the work.

scope.description: The text and apparatus printed in Anglia 32 and encoded by U-M
Transcription of Harley MS 2274, historical fols. 35a-46b, now ff. 35r-46v

subjects.label: Life of the Virgin Mary from her birth through the Assumption
Sacred weeping, tears, and instruction in compassion

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English devotional poem in which a penitential speaker, kneeling before an image of the Virgin with the dead Christ, enters a visionary state and hears Mary narrate her life from childhood through Christ's Passion and her own Assumption. Each twelve-line stanza turns Mary's joy, fear, or grief into instruction in sacred tears through the refrain 'Who can not wepe come lerne att me.' CME00093 also retains Robert Max Garrett's modern English preface and manuscript notes as editorial paratext.

The encoded poem has 72 numbered stanzas and 864 verse-line elements. Garrett prints manuscript-folio milestones from historical 35a through 46b, now expressed as ff. 35r-46v, 36 apparatus notes, and a variable refrain. Four modern-English prose paragraphs preceding the poem describe the Harley witness, stanza and rhyme form, language, and literary qualities.

Garrett transcribed only Harley MS 2274. DIMEV 3777 identifies three additional witnesses: a 76-stanza Bodley 423 text, a three-stanza Corpus Christi College Oxford 261 fragment, and a 52-stanza Trinity College Dublin 160 text. Their readings, dates, and image terms are not transferred into Garrett's direct source chain.

notes: Garrett used this as the article title, and DIMEV reports that the Harley scribe wrote the same Latin title in the upper margin of fol. 35r. It is retained as the established discovery title rather than treated as proof of an authorial title.
Garrett's fuller printed heading means approximately 'On the art of weeping: a personification of the Blessed Virgin.' It is not identified as the complete manuscript rubric.
titles.value: De arte lacrimandi. Prosopopaeia B. Virginie

notes: DIMEV 1.0 prints 'My sinful language to abate'; Garrett, the XML, and the BL incipit identify 'living/lyuyng,' so the DIMEV wording is retained as an open data-quality question rather than adopted here.
titles.value: Now late me thought I wolde begynne / My synfull lyuyng to abate

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Fifteenth century; copied in Bodleian MS Bodley 423 by approximately 1430-1440
work.regionDisplay: England; precise place of composition and dialect region unresolved

subjects.label: Life of the Virgin Mary from her birth through the Assumption
Sacred weeping, tears, and instruction in compassion

work.summary: An anonymous Middle English devotional poem in which a penitential speaker, kneeling before an image of the Virgin with the dead Christ, enters a visionary state and hears Mary narrate her life from childhood through Christ's Passion and her own Assumption. Each twelve-line stanza turns Mary's joy, fear, or grief into instruction in sacred tears through the refrain 'Who can not wepe come lerne att me.' CME00093 also retains Robert Max Garrett's modern English preface and manuscript notes as editorial paratext.
